findings.description: Glorious textbook SQL injection: the email is f-string-interpolated straight into the query, so any user can type a quote and own your whole users table.
The expiry check is inverted: live sessions hit the 'expired' branch and get deleted+rejected, while genuinely expired tokens fall through and are happily accepted as valid.
LOCKOUT_MINUTES is lovingly defined on line 17 and then never used, so a locked account stays locked until the heat death of the universe with no time-based reset.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated and returns naive datetimes here, line 90/147, guaranteeing a TypeError the instant anyone compares against a timezone-aware value.
Sessions live only in an in-memory dict, so every restart logs everyone out and multi-worker deployments hand each process its own private, incoherent session store.
find_user_by_email cheerfully SELECTs the entire users table and linear-scans it in Python to find one row the database could have indexed in O(log n).
Unknown-email path returns before hashing, line 73, creating a timing oracle that lets an attacker enumerate which emails actually exist.
The failed-attempts increment and the read of failed_attempts are not in a single transaction, so concurrent logins can race the lockout counter and blow past MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS.
Using typing.Optional in 2026 instead of X | None is a quaint museum-piece choice but offends nobody except the linter.
